title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: dependency-free collection simple functions cleaning rectangular data. package allows detect, count replace values discard rows/columns using predicate function. addition, provides tools check conditions return informative error messages.
code: To cite arkhe in publications use:    Frerebeau N (2024). _arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data_.   Université Bordeaux Montaigne, Pessac, France.   doi:10.5281/zenodo.3526659 <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3526659>,   R package version 1.6.0, <https://packages.tesselle.org/arkhe/>.  A BibTeX entry for LaTeX users is    @Manual{,     author = {Nicolas Frerebeau},     title = {{arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data}},     year = {2024},     organization = {Université Bordeaux Montaigne},     address = {Pessac, France},     note = {R package version 1.6.0},     url = {https://packages.tesselle.org/arkhe/},     doi = {10.5281/zenodo.3526659},   }  This package is a part of the tesselle project <https://www.tesselle.org>.

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: can install released version arkhe CRAN : development version GitHub :
code: install.packages("arkhe") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tesselle/arkhe")

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
code: ## Load the package library(arkhe)  ## Create a matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    6    7    1   NA   NA #> [2,]    6    3    1    7    1 #> [3,]    9    5    6    4   10 #> [4,]   10    9    6   NA    5 #> [5,]    6   10   10    7    9  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 2 0 0 1 0 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 5 5 5 3 4  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    6    3    1    7    1 #> [2,]    9    5    6    4   10 #> [3,]    6   10   10    7    9 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    6    7    1 #> [2,]    6    3    1 #> [3,]    9    5    6 #> [4,]   10    9    6 #> [5,]    6   10   10  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    6    7    1    0    0 #> [2,]    6    3    1    7    1 #> [3,]    9    5    6    4   10 #> [4,]   10    9    6    0    5 #> [5,]    6   10   10    7    9

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: Please note arkhe project released Contributor Code Conduct. contributing project, agree abide terms.

what: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
code: append_rownames(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame append_rownames(x, after = 0, remove = TRUE, var = "rownames")

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
text: x data.frame. ... Currently used. length-one numeric vector specifying subscript, row names appended. remove logical scalar: row names removed? var character string giving name column use row names.

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
code: X <- data.frame(   x = 1:5,   y = 6:10,   z = LETTERS[1:5] )  ## Assign column to row names (Y <- assign_rownames(X, 3)) #>   x  y #> A 1  6 #> B 2  7 #> C 3  8 #> D 4  9 #> E 5 10  ## Append row names to data.frame (Z <- append_rownames(Y)) #>   rownames x  y #> 1        A 1  6 #> 2        B 2  7 #> 3        C 3  8 #> 4        D 4  9 #> 5        E 5 10

title: Deprecated Functions in arkhe — arkhe-deprecated

title: Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign

Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign
code: X <- data.frame(   x = 1:5,   y = 6:10,   z = LETTERS[1:5] )  ## Assign column to row names (Y <- assign_rownames(X, 3)) #>   x  y #> A 1  6 #> B 2  7 #> C 3  8 #> D 4  9 #> E 5 10  ## Append row names to data.frame (Z <- append_rownames(Y)) #>   rownames x  y #> 1        A 1  6 #> 2        B 2  7 #> 3        C 3  8 #> 4        D 4  9 #> 5        E 5 10

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
code: bootstrap(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric bootstrap(object, do, n, ..., f = NULL)

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: object numeric vector. ... Extra arguments passed . function takes object argument returns single numeric value. n non-negative integer giving number bootstrap replications. f function takes single numeric vector (result ) argument.

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: f NULL (default), bootstrap() returns named numeric vector following elements: original observed value applied object. mean bootstrap estimate mean . bias bootstrap estimate bias . error bootstrap estimate standard error . f function, bootstrap() returns result f applied n values .

code: check_class(object, conditions)

text: object object error messages related. conditions list condition messages.

title: Class Diagnostic — check_class
text: Throw error conditions non-zero length, invisibly returns TRUE .

title: Draw a Circle — circle
code: circle(x, y, radius, ..., n = 100)

title: Draw a Circle — circle
text: x, y length-one numeric vector giving coordinates center circle. radius length-one numeric vector giving radius circle. ... parameters passed graphics::polygon(). n length-integer vector specifying number vertices draw circle.

title: Draw a Circle — circle
code: if (FALSE) { plot(NA, xlim = c(-1, 1), ylim = c(-1, 1),      axes = FALSE, ann = FALSE, asp = 1) circle(0, 0, 0.5) }

title: Remove Leading/Trailing Whitespace — clean_whitespace
code: clean_whitespace(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame clean_whitespace(x, which = c("both", "left", "right"), squish = TRUE)  # S4 method for matrix clean_whitespace(x, which = c("both", "left", "right"), squish = TRUE)

title: Remove Leading/Trailing Whitespace — clean_whitespace
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... Currently used. character string specifying whether remove leading trailing whitespace (default), leading ("left") trailing ("right"). squish logical scalar: internal whitespace replaced single space?

title: Remove Leading/Trailing Whitespace — clean_whitespace
code: x <- data.frame(   A = c("  Both ", "  Left", "Right  "),   B = 1:3 )  clean_whitespace(x, which = "both") #>       A B #> 1  Both 1 #> 2  Left 2 #> 3 Right 3 clean_whitespace(x, which = "left") #>        A B #> 1  Both  1 #> 2   Left 2 #> 3 Right  3 clean_whitespace(x, which = "right") #>       A B #> 1  Both 1 #> 2  Left 2 #> 3 Right 3

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
code: compact(x, ...)  compact_cols(x, ...)  compact_rows(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY compact(x, margin = 1, na.rm = FALSE, verbose = getOption("arkhe.verbose"))  # S4 method for ANY compact_cols(x, na.rm = FALSE, verbose = getOption("arkhe.verbose"))  # S4 method for ANY compact_rows(x, na.rm = FALSE, verbose = getOption("arkhe.verbose"))

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... Currently used. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). na.rm logical scalar: NA values stripped computation proceeds? verbose logical scalar: R report extra information progress?

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
text: row/column empty contains zeros (type numeric) zero length character strings (type character).

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
code: ## Create a data.frame X <- data.frame(A = 0, B = 1:5, C = 6, D = "", F = letters[1:5]) X #>   A B C D F #> 1 0 1 6   a #> 2 0 2 6   b #> 3 0 3 6   c #> 4 0 4 6   d #> 5 0 5 6   e  ## Remove empty columns compact(X, margin = 2) #>   B C F #> 1 1 6 a #> 2 2 6 b #> 3 3 6 c #> 4 4 6 d #> 5 5 6 e

title: Concatenate — concat
code: x %+% y

title: Conditions — conditions
text: throw_error() stops execution current expression executes error action. throw_warning() generates warning message. catch_conditions() catch_message() handles unusual conditions.

title: Conditions — conditions
code: throw_error(.subclass, message, call = NULL, ...)  throw_warning(.subclass, message, call = NULL, ...)  catch_message(expr)  catch_conditions(expr)

title: Conditions — conditions
text: .subclass character string specifying class message returned. message character string specifying message returned. call call. ... Extra arguments. expr expression evaluated.

what: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: Computes Wald interval proportion desired level significance.

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
code: confidence_binomial(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric confidence_binomial(   object,   n,   level = 0.95,   method = "wald",   corrected = FALSE )

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: object numeric vector giving number success. ... Currently used. n length-one numeric vector giving number trials. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. Must single number \(0\) \(1\). method character string specifying method used. unambiguous substring can used. corrected logical scalar: continuity correction used? used method "wald".

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: length-two numeric vector giving lower upper confidence limits.

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
code: ## Confidence interval for a mean x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence_mean(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence_mean(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459   ## Confidence interval for a propotion confidence_binomial(118, n = 236) #>     lower     upper  #> 0.4362086 0.5637914   x <- c(35, 74, 22, 69) confidence_multinomial(x) #>           lower     upper #> [1,] 0.12234021 0.2276598 #> [2,] 0.30308797 0.4369120 #> [3,] 0.06663649 0.1533635 #> [4,] 0.27911853 0.4108815

what: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean

code: confidence_mean(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric confidence_mean(object, level = 0.95, type = c("student", "normal"))

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
text: object numeric vector. ... Currently used. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. Must single number \(0\) \(1\). type character string giving type confidence interval returned. must one "student" (default) "normal". unambiguous substring can given.

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
text: length-two numeric vector giving lower upper confidence limits.

code: ## Confidence interval for a mean x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence_mean(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence_mean(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459   ## Confidence interval for a propotion confidence_binomial(118, n = 236) #>     lower     upper  #> 0.4362086 0.5637914   x <- c(35, 74, 22, 69) confidence_multinomial(x) #>           lower     upper #> [1,] 0.12234021 0.2276598 #> [2,] 0.30308797 0.4369120 #> [3,] 0.06663649 0.1533635 #> [4,] 0.27911853 0.4108815

Must single number \(0\) \(1\). method character string specifying method used. unambiguous substring can used. corrected logical scalar: continuity correction used? used method "wald".

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
code: ## Confidence interval for a mean x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence_mean(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence_mean(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459   ## Confidence interval for a propotion confidence_binomial(118, n = 236) #>     lower     upper  #> 0.4362086 0.5637914   x <- c(35, 74, 22, 69) confidence_multinomial(x) #>           lower     upper #> [1,] 0.12234021 0.2276598 #> [2,] 0.30308797 0.4369120 #> [3,] 0.06663649 0.1533635 #> [4,] 0.27911853 0.4108815

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? na.rm logical scalar: NA values stripped computation proceeds?

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
Count Values Using a Predicate — count
Count Values Using a Predicate — count
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    2   NA    4    6   NA #> [2,]    2    3    8    9    2 #> [3,]    8    1   10    9   10 #> [4,]    2    8   10    3   10 #> [5,]   NA    1   10    6    3  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 2 0 0 0 1 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> [1] 4 4 5 5 4

title: Data Description — describe
text: describe() called side-effects. Invisibly returns x.

title: Data Description — describe
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(0:9, 15, TRUE), nrow = 3, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:15, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA  ## Sparsity sparsity(X) #> [1] 0  ## Quick description describe(X) #> 3 observations with 5 variables: #> * 5 numeric variables. #> * 0 categorial variables. #> * 0 binary variables. #>  #> Data checking: #> * 0% of numeric values are zero. #> * 0 variables with no variance. #>  #> Missing values: #> * 3 observations (100%) containing missing values. #> * 2 variables (40%) containing missing values.

what: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
text: Finds rows/columns array-like object using predicate function.

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
code: detect(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY detect(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE, all = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE, ...)

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered. na.rm logical scalar: NA values stripped computation proceeds?

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    8    9    8   NA    7 #> [2,]    6    8    8    7   10 #> [3,]   10    2    3   NA   10 #> [4,]    1    7   NA   10    3 #> [5,]    8    1    2    3    1  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE

code: ## HDR of the Old Faithful eruption times interval_hdr(faithful$eruptions) #>         start      end    p #> [1,] 1.307160 2.837942 0.33 #> [2,] 3.150567 5.295819 0.62

title: Highest Density Regions — interval_hdr
text: x numeric vector giving coordinates points density estimated. y numeric vector giving estimated density values. y missing x numeric vector, density estimates computed x. ... arguments passed stats::density(). level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level.

title: Highest Density Regions — interval_hdr
text: three-columns numeric  matrix giving lower upper boundaries HPD interval associated probabilities.

title: Highest Density Regions — interval_hdr
text: Hyndman, R. J. (1996). Computing graphing highest density regions. American Statistician, 50: 120-126. doi:10.2307/2684423 .

title: Highest Density Regions — interval_hdr
code: ## HDR of the Old Faithful eruption times interval_hdr(faithful$eruptions) #>         start      end    p #> [1,] 1.307160 2.837942 0.33 #> [2,] 3.150567 5.295819 0.62

title: Jackknife Estimation — jackknife
text: f NULL (default), jackknife() returns named numeric vector following elements: original observed value applied object. mean jackknife estimate mean . bias jackknife estimate bias . error jackknife estimate standard error . f function, jackknife() returns result f applied leave-one-values .

what: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
text: Keeps rows/columns array-like object using predicate function.

title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
code: keep(x, ...)  keep_cols(x, ...)  keep_rows(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY keep(   x,   f,   margin = 1,   negate = FALSE,   all = FALSE,   na.rm = FALSE,   verbose = getOption("arkhe.verbose"),   ... )  # S4 method for ANY keep_rows(   x,   f,   negate = FALSE,   all = FALSE,   na.rm = FALSE,   verbose = getOption("arkhe.verbose"),   ... )  # S4 method for ANY keep_cols(   x,   f,   negate = FALSE,   all = FALSE,   na.rm = FALSE,   verbose = getOption("arkhe.verbose"),   ... )

title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered. na.rm logical scalar: NA values stripped computation proceeds? verbose logical scalar: R report extra information progress?

title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    5    2    6    8 #> [2,]    6   10    1    8    8 #> [3,]   NA    5   10    6    3 #> [4,]    8   NA    3    4    5 #> [5,]    8   10   NA    4    4  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    5    2    6    8 #> [2,]    6   10    1    8    8 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    6    8 #> [2,]    8    8 #> [3,]    6    3 #> [4,]    4    5 #> [5,]    4    4

title: Matrix Predicates — predicate-matrix
text: is_square() checks matrix square. is_symmetric() checks matrix symmetric.

title: Matrix Predicates — predicate-matrix
code: is_square(x)  is_symmetric(x)

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: Check numeric objects: is_zero() checks object contains zeros. is_odd() is_even() check number odd even, respectively. is_positive() is_negative check object contains (strictly) positive negative numbers. is_whole() checks object contains whole numbers.

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
code: is_zero(x, tolerance = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps), ...)  is_odd(x, ...)  is_even(x, ...)  is_positive(x, strict = FALSE, ...)  is_negative(x, strict = FALSE, ...)  is_whole(x, tolerance = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps), ...)

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: x numeric object tested. tolerance numeric scalar giving tolerance check within. ... Currently used. strict logical scalar: strict inequality used?

title: Numeric Trend Predicates — predicate-trend
text: Check numeric objects: is_constant() checks equality among elements vector. is_increasing() is_decreasing() check sequence numbers monotonically increasing decreasing, respectively.

title: Numeric Trend Predicates — predicate-trend
code: is_constant(x, tolerance = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps), na.rm = FALSE)  is_increasing(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_decreasing(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_greater(x, y, strict = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)  is_lower(x, y, strict = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)

title: Numeric Trend Predicates — predicate-trend
text: x, y numeric object tested. tolerance numeric scalar giving tolerance check within. na.rm logical scalar: missing values (including NaN) omitted? strict logical scalar: strict inequality used?

title: Type Predicates — predicate-type
code: is_list(x)  is_atomic(x)  is_vector(x)  is_numeric(x)  is_integer(x)  is_double(x)  is_character(x)  is_logical(x)  is_error(x)  is_warning(x)  is_message(x)

title: Utility Predicates — predicate-utils
text: is_empty() checks object empty (zero-length dimensions). has_length() checks long object. has_names() checks object named. has_duplicates() checks object duplicated elements. has_missing() has_infinite() check object contains missing infinite values.

title: Utility Predicates — predicate-utils
code: has_length(x, n = NULL)  is_empty(x)  has_names(x, names = NULL)  has_missing(x)  has_infinite(x)  is_unique(x, tolerance = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps), na.rm = FALSE)  has_duplicates(x)

title: Utility Predicates — predicate-utils
text: x vector tested. n length-one numeric vector specifying length test x . NULL, returns TRUE x length greater zero, FALSE otherwise. names character vector specifying names test x . NULL, returns TRUE x names, FALSE otherwise. tolerance numeric scalar giving tolerance check within (numeric vector). na.rm logical scalar: missing values (including NaN) omitted?

title: Remove Rows/Columns with Empty String — remove_empty
Remove Rows/Columns with Empty String — remove_empty
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(LETTERS, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add empty string k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- "" X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,] "G"  "B"  "B"  ""   "C"  #> [2,] "B"  "C"  "B"  "S"  "O"  #> [3,] "P"  "O"  "U"  "L"  "I"  #> [4,] "S"  "N"  ""   "Y"  "E"  #> [5,] "M"  "S"  ""   "W"  "E"   ## Remove rows with empty strings remove_empty(X, margin = 1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,] "B"  "C"  "B"  "S"  "O"  #> [2,] "P"  "O"  "U"  "L"  "I"   ## Replace empty strings replace_empty(X, value = "XXX") #>      [,1] [,2] [,3]  [,4]  [,5] #> [1,] "G"  "B"  "B"   "XXX" "C"  #> [2,] "B"  "C"  "B"   "S"   "O"  #> [3,] "P"  "O"  "U"   "L"   "I"  #> [4,] "S"  "N"  "XXX" "Y"   "E"  #> [5,] "M"  "S"  "XXX" "W"   "E"

code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(LETTERS, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add empty string k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- "" X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,] "O"  "D"  "D"  "Z"  "Q"  #> [2,] ""   "G"  "L"  "G"  ""   #> [3,] "C"  "J"  ""   "U"  "D"  #> [4,] "I"  "S"  "Z"  "T"  "A"  #> [5,] "N"  "B"  "B"  "A"  "Q"   ## Remove rows with empty strings remove_empty(X, margin = 1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,] "O"  "D"  "D"  "Z"  "Q"  #> [2,] "I"  "S"  "Z"  "T"  "A"  #> [3,] "N"  "B"  "B"  "A"  "Q"   ## Replace empty strings replace_empty(X, value = "XXX") #>      [,1]  [,2] [,3]  [,4] [,5]  #> [1,] "O"   "D"  "D"   "Z"  "Q"   #> [2,] "XXX" "G"  "L"   "G"  "XXX" #> [3,] "C"   "J"  "XXX" "U"  "D"   #> [4,] "I"   "S"  "Z"   "T"  "A"   #> [5,] "N"   "B"  "B"   "A"  "Q"

title: Sparsity — sparsity
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(0:9, 15, TRUE), nrow = 3, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:15, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA  ## Sparsity sparsity(X) #> [1] 0.06666667  ## Quick description describe(X) #> 3 observations with 5 variables: #> * 5 numeric variables. #> * 0 categorial variables. #> * 0 binary variables. #>  #> Data checking: #> * 7% of numeric values are zero. #> * 0 variables with no variance. #>  #> Missing values: #> * 2 observations (67%) containing missing values. #> * 3 variables (60%) containing missing values.
